summary: Judith Butler discusses the performativity of public assembly, the shared condition of precarity, and the ethical necessity of cohabitation. She argues against basing ethical obligations on mere identification, advocating instead for unexpected alliances, collective responsibility, and a nonviolent commitment to building a common world despite our differences.

principles.statement: Ethical obligations must not rely solely on identification or likeness.
principles.rationale: Relying on identification restricts sympathy to those who are like us. We must maintain obligations to those who are different, those we fear, or those who have injured us because the world is given to us in common.
principles.source_quote: This is why I do not think that global obligations can rest on identification, even expanded or expanding identifications; they have to claim us quite regardless of whether or not we feel love or sympathy, for the simple reason that the world is given to us in common and that without each other the world is not given.

principles.statement: We must take collective responsibility for overcoming conditions of induced precarity.
principles.rationale: Our persistence and flourishing are inherently social. We depend on networks of care and infrastructures, and when they fail, we fail.
principles.source_quote: This social character of our persistence and our possible flourishing means that we have to take collective responsibility for overcoming conditions of induced precarity.

principles.statement: Action is always implicitly plural and reciprocal.
principles.rationale: We are thrown into a world of others before we make any decisions. Our agency must take a non-egological point of departure, recognizing our prior entanglement with others.
principles.source_quote: It means that action is always implicitly plural and reciprocal, even when that is not the case in existing circumstances.

principles.statement: We must pause and reflect on the means of our resistance, prioritizing nonviolence.
principles.rationale: Building a world together requires questioning violent means, even when acting out of righteous rage, because the means must align with the world we want to build.
principles.source_quote: If the means are violent, how are they justified? I would like to persuade people who are in righteous rage that the turn to violence is not what they finally want, since at stake is not just finding a way to react immediately and legibly but building a world together.

mental_models.name: Embodied Performativity
mental_models.description: Viewing public demonstrations and the physical assembly of bodies as a form of political expression and demand-making, especially when speech is absent or marginalized.
mental_models.example: Demonstrations opposing evictions in Barcelona or police brutality in the United States, which document the failures of justice through the physical presence of bodies.

mental_models.name: Social Freedom (The "Between")
mental_models.description: The understanding that freedom is not merely personal liberty, but a sphere co-produced by people living together. The world is only known and given because there is a "you" to share it with.
mental_models.example: Arendt's and Buber's concepts where the "I" only knows its world because there is a "you" who has consciousness of that world.

heuristics: We are worldless without one another.
Decision only happens in the context of a prior entanglement.
We live together because we have no choice.

signature_quotes.text: We are worldless without one another.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing how the world is never given to us alone, but always in the company of others.

signature_quotes.text: If the self is the basis of sympathy, our sympathy will be restricted to those who are like us.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why global ethical obligations cannot rest merely on feelings of identification.

signature_quotes.text: Without the freedom of movement and assembly, we lose our very character as a democracy.
signature_quotes.context: Defending public demonstrations against being shut down under the guise of 'security' or 'riot' control.

signature_quotes.text: Decision only happens in the context of a prior entanglement.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining that we are thrown into a world of others long before we decide to throw our lot in with them.

signature_quotes.text: I am open to a world that acts on me in ways that cannot be fully predicted or controlled in advance, and something about my openness is not, strictly speaking, under my control.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on the vulnerability and exposure of having a body in the world.

anti_patterns.description: Basing ethical obligations solely on feelings of identification or likeness.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It restricts sympathy to those who are like us and thwarts our ability to respond ethically to alterity, those we fear, or those who are distant.

anti_patterns.description: Assuming freedom is only a matter of personal liberty.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores that personal liberty relies on a co-produced sphere of social freedom; freedom happens as a relation between us.

anti_patterns.description: Turning to violence in righteous rage to oppose systemic issues like racism.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It undermines the ultimate goal of building a shared world together; the means must align with the world we want to build.
